context.organization_description: 120-person mid-market consulting firm, 12 years old, recently expanded from 1 office to 3 (NYC, SF, Austin)
key_dynamics: Competing philosophies: founders (in-person traditionalists) vs. younger partners (hybrid advocates)
Geographic divide: SF/Austin teams more remote-oriented; NYC headquarters culturally in-office
Unspoken tension: senior partners worry about junior talent development and mentorship; junior staff worry about overwork and surveillance
Financial pressure: real estate costs vs. client expectations about junior team availability
known_tensions: Generational: Partners (avg age 48) vs. Consultants (avg age 29)
Tenure-based: 5+ year veterans more compliant; <2 year staff more resistant
Function-based: Client-facing staff push back on in-office more; Operations/Finance largely in-office

interview_objectives.research_topic: Hybrid work policy adoption, experienced barriers, and impact on retention and team dynamics
interview_objectives.primary_research_question: Why is the 3-day-in-office mandate not being consistently followed, and what would actually drive compliance or policy revision?
key_themes_to_probe: Perceived purpose of in-office time (mentorship? culture? surveillance?)
Client work realities (can it actually be done better in-office?)
Who is exempt or non-compliant, and why
Generational/tenure differences in how people experience the policy
What would make people want to come in, vs. what forces them
interview_objectives.depth_criteria: Move beyond stated compliance reasons into emotional drivers, unspoken concerns, and what people actually think leadership believes vs. what they say

required_topics_per_interview: Current hybrid work arrangement (de facto, not de jure)
Perception of policy purpose and fairness
Impact on their actual work (productivity, collaboration, client relationships)
Social/cultural experience of office vs. remote
Retention sentiment and alternative job consideration
What leadership 'actually' believes (perception vs. stated reasoning)
What would change their behavior or the policy
interview_output_structure.depth_expectation: 30-45 min interviews; allow for contradiction, nuance, off-the-record thinking
interview_output_structure.per_interview_deliverable: Transcript + coded themes + direct quotes capturing the interviewee's perspective and contradictions

output_sections: Executive Summary: Key tensions and behavioral reality
Segmented Findings: By role (partner/senior/junior), geography, tenure
The Compliance Gap: Where policy and practice diverge and why
Hidden Costs: Retention risk, recruitment impact, cultural friction
What Actually Drives Behavior: Incentives, social proof, trust
Recommendations: Policy revision options and conditions for adoption
focus_areas: Surface the generational and geographic divides without taking sides
Distinguish between stated policy rationale and what people actually believe
Make visible what's currently invisible (who's leaving, who's struggling, who's gaming it)
Offer pathways for leadership to address underlying concerns, not just mandate compliance
